title: Accumulating Positive Emotions: Long Term Accumulate positive emotions in the long term to build a “life worth living.” That is, make changes in your life so that positive events will occur in the future.

pages.text:
252
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
☐
Emotion Regulation Handout 17   (Emotion Regulation Worksheets 9, 11–11b, 13)
Accumulating Positive Emotions: Long Term
Accumulate positive emotions in the long term
to build a “life worth living.”
That is, make changes in your life so that positive events will occur in the future.
Step 1. Avoid avoiding.
Start now to do what is needed to build the life you want. If you are not sure about
what to do, follow the steps below.
Step 2. Identify values that are important to you.
Ask: What values are really important to me in my life?
Examples: Be productive; be part of a group; treat others well; be physically fit.
Step 3. Identify one value to work on now.
Ask: What is really important to me, right now, to work on in my life?
Example: Be productive.
Step 4. Identify a few goals related to this value.
Ask: What specific goals can I work on that will make this value part of my life?
Examples: Get a job where I can do something useful.
      Be more active keeping up with important tasks at home.
      Find a volunteer job that will use skills I already have.
Step 5. Choose one goal to work on now.
Do pros and cons, if necessary, to select a goal to work on now.
Example: Get a job where I can do something useful.
Step 6. Identify small action steps toward your goal.
Ask: What small steps can I take to get to my goal?
Examples: Visit places and look for job openings on the Internet in my area.
      Submit applications for jobs at places I want to work.
      Write résumé.
      Check out benefits at places I might want to work.
Step 7. Take one action step now.
Example: Go on Internet and check for jobs in my area.

Emotion Regulation Handout 17   (Emotion Regulation Worksheets 9, 11–11b, 13)
Accumulating Positive Emotions: Long Term
Accumulate positive emotions in the long term
to build a “life worth living.”
That is, make changes in your life so that positive events will occur in the future.
Step 1. Avoid avoiding.
Start now to do what is needed to build the life you want. If you are not sure about
what to do, follow the steps below.
Step 2. Identify values that are important to you.
Ask: What values are really important to me in my life?
Examples: Be productive; be part of a group; treat others well; be physically fit.
Step 3. Identify one value to work on now.
Ask: What is really important to me, right now, to work on in my life?
Example: Be productive.
Step 4. Identify a few goals related to this value.
Ask: What specific goals can I work on that will make this value part of my life?
Examples: Get a job where I can do something useful.
      Be more active keeping up with important tasks at home.
      Find a volunteer job that will use skills I already have.
Step 5. Choose one goal to work on now.
Do pros and cons, if necessary, to select a goal to work on now.
Example: Get a job where I can do something useful.
Step 6. Identify small action steps toward your goal.
Ask: What small steps can I take to get to my goal?
Examples: Visit places and look for job openings on the Internet in my area.
      Submit applications for jobs at places I want to work.
      Write résumé.
      Check out benefits at places I might want to work.
Step 7. Take one action step now.
Example: Go on Internet and check for jobs in my area.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Accumulating Positive Emotions: Long Term Accumulate positive emotions in the long term to build a “life worth living.” That is, make changes in your life so that positive events will occur in the future.
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Emotion work starts with the body and access needs. Hunger, pain, oxygen, sleep attacks, MCAS reactions, trauma cues, and exhaustion count.
tiny_steps: Check body basics: breathing, pain, food/water, meds, temperature, sensory load.
Lower the demand by one size.
Choose soothe, solve, pause, or ask for help.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Allow anger and grief without turning them into shame.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not demand immediate calm during respiratory distress, pain flares, dissociation, or overload.

ourspace_shared_revision_metadata.copyright_note: Extracted from user-provided PDF: DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Respect source copyright and purchaser-use limits.
